artifacts.content:
Hi Mum,

I know you’re on nights at St George this week so I thought I’d write everything down properly instead of trying to catch you between your shifts.

I picked up the new sertraline today – still the 25 mg tablets like Dr Priya Desai set up at the Adolescent Mental Health Clinic at St George Hospital. It’s weird to think it’s been about nine months already. I haven’t had a full‑on panic attack in a while, except that one in the assembly hall at Georges River College Oatley Senior Campus last term when everyone was packed in and it got super loud. It lasted maybe 10–15 minutes but the breathing exercises she taught me actually helped.

I also wanted to remind you about the snacks thing. Ever since I fainted at MOBC in September during Sunday service (still so embarrassing), I’ve been better about eating breakfast, but can you grab some more muesli bars and those electrolyte sachets from Woolies? The ambos from St George said it was probably just a vasovagal episode from low blood sugar plus the stress from Year 10 exams, but I don’t want a repeat. If I’m rostered on kids’ ministry for more than 3 hours, I’ll keep one in my pocket like you said.

Anyway, I put the sertraline box in the bathroom cupboard next to the loratadine so I don’t forget it at night. I’ll also set a reminder on my phone. Text me if you want me to start dinner tomorrow before you get home.

Love,
Sophia

Hi Uncle David,

Sorry it’s been ages. School and the café have been hectic, but I wanted to update you on a few things (and also ask for laptop advice later!).

First, about Ngoai – I still remember visiting her at Bankstown Hospital back in August 2022 when she got the type 2 diabetes diagnosis. My GP at Mortdale Medical Centre finally decided to do extra blood tests for me in June 2024 because of the family history. The fasting blood sugar was normal, but he said I’m “at higher risk,” so Mum has gone full health‑police mode. We’re basically on a no‑added‑sugar rule at home and she checks my lunchbox every morning before I leave for Georges River College Oatley Senior Campus. No more sneaky Milo.

On top of that, I’ve been getting these tension headaches and neck pain since Term 2 this year. Between late‑night study and weekend shifts at Riverside Bean Café in Mortdale Plaza, my shoulders are like rocks. My GP referred me to a physio, Daniel Ho, in July 2024. I see him every second Thursday after school. He even wrote a letter saying I shouldn’t be lifting heavy crates at work or standing for more than 4 hours straight, which Maria and Sarah at the café have been pretty nice about.

I’m trying to be good about stretching and not living on instant noodles. If you have any easy, non‑boring healthy lunch ideas that still pass Mum’s inspection, please send them.

Also, when you have time, can you recommend a decent second‑hand laptop brand/model for design stuff? I’m saving my café money.

Love,
Sophia

metadata.subject: Ngoai, health stuff, and laptop questions

artifacts.content:
Private journal – 18 October 2024

Today was rough.

We had another whole‑school assembly in the big hall at GRC Oatley and I could feel it starting before we even sat down – the buzzing noise, people shifting, that weird echo. I ended up having a 10–15 minute panic episode again. Heart racing, hands shaking, felt like I couldn’t breathe properly. I kept reminding myself of what Dr Priya Desai at the Adolescent Mental Health Clinic at St George Hospital said back in May 2023: slow breathing, name five things I can see, don’t skip my sertraline. I’m still on 25 mg every night and I *know* it’s helping overall, but assemblies are still a trigger.

On top of that, my neck and shoulders were already tight from staying up late finishing the History migration project. The tension headaches from Term 2 never really went away, even though Daniel Ho (physio from Mortdale Medical Centre) has me doing stretches every second Thursday after school. He keeps reminding me about the letter he wrote saying I shouldn’t stand more than 4 hours at Riverside Bean Café or lift heavy crates. I feel guilty when the others are lugging milk and I’m stuck on counter.

Need to:
- Keep up sertraline at night, no skipping.
- Book next physio with Daniel Ho.
- Ask Year Adviser if there’s any way to sit near an exit during assemblies so I don’t feel trapped.
- Actually sleep before midnight at least 3 nights a week.

I hate feeling “weak,” but I also don’t want to end up back with the ambos like at MOBC.

Event: Physio – Daniel Ho (neck + headaches)
Date: Thursday 2024-08-15
Time: 4:30 pm – 5:15 pm
Location: Mortdale Medical Centre – Physio rooms

Details/notes:
- Ongoing tension headaches + neck pain since Term 2 2024 from late‑night study + weekend shifts at Riverside Bean Café, Mortdale Plaza.
- This is one of the regular “every second Thursday” appointments Daniel set up after the GP referral in July 2024.
- Bring school backpack to show how heavy it is – ask if that’s making things worse.
- Re‑print or re‑scan Daniel’s letter for work: says I shouldn’t lift heavy crates (esp. milk/soft drink deliveries) or stand for more than 4 hours straight on shift.
- Mention that I’m also on sertraline 25 mg at night from Dr Desai at the Adolescent Mental Health Clinic at St George Hospital for social anxiety + panic attacks in crowded places (like the GRC Oatley assembly hall). Ask if the muscle tension might be partly from anxiety.
- Ask for specific stretches I can do behind the counter at the café and during breaks at school so I’m not super obvious.
- Check if it’s okay to keep doing guitar at MOBC youth group or if I need to adjust posture.

Reminder: leave school at 3:30 pm, catch 958 bus from Oatley to Mortdale. Take water bottle + small snack (no repeat of the MOBC fainting episode).

Chat with Hannah (MOBC youth)

Sophia: hey are you still okay to swap kids’ ministry with me this Sunday?

Hannah: yeah that’s fine! you alright tho? you looked pale last week

Sophia: lol yeah, just paranoid after that September thing when I fainted during the Sunday service at MOBC. Remember how the paramedics from St George Hospital came and checked my blood sugar + blood pressure in front of everyone 😭 they said it was probably a vasovagal episode from not eating + stress from Year 10 exams at GRC Oatley.

Hannah: yesss I remember, your mum was so worried

Sophia: yeah now Mum makes me carry muesli bars + those electrolyte sachets if I’m on roster more than 3 hours. tbh it’s fair, I keep “forgetting” breakfast.

Hannah: I’ll bring extra snacks too dw

Sophia: thanks. also cramps are starting again ugh. I finally went to Family Planning NSW in Ashfield back in March and started a low‑dose pill in April. It’s helped but I still get one really bad day each cycle where I can’t stand more than like 2–3 hours without wanting to curl up with a heat pack + ibuprofen.

Hannah: that sucks :( do you want me to tell Mel you might need more sitting jobs this Sunday?

Sophia: maybe, esp if it lines up with that bad day. between that + my social anxiety/panic stuff from the assemblies at school I feel like my body is constantly being dramatic haha.

Hannah: not dramatic, just human. I got you.

Hi Sarah,

Hope your week’s going okay and the machine isn’t playing up again.

I wanted to let you know I’ve finally seen a physio about the headaches/neck pain I’ve been getting from school and work. My GP at Mortdale Medical Centre referred me to a physiotherapist, Daniel Ho, in July 2024. I’ve been seeing him every second Thursday after school. He wrote a letter (I can bring a copy in on Saturday) saying I shouldn’t be lifting heavy crates at Riverside Bean Café or standing for more than 4 hours straight.

He thinks the combination of late‑night study for Year 10 and my weekend shifts is what’s causing the tension headaches. I also have mild social anxiety and panic attacks – I’m on 25 mg sertraline at night from Dr Priya Desai at the Adolescent Mental Health Clinic at St George Hospital. Crowded places like our Saturday rush or the assembly hall at Georges River College Oatley Senior Campus can set me off if I’m already tired or in pain, so we’re trying to reduce extra physical stress where possible.

I still really want to keep working here and I’m happy to do counter, dishes, wiping tables, and restocking the pastry cabinet. I just probably shouldn’t be the one hauling the big milk crates or standing at the machine nonstop on the longer shifts. If we can roster me more on front counter/food prep and maybe shorter stretches on the machine, that would help heaps.

Thanks for being understanding about all this. I appreciate you and Maria being flexible while I sort my body/brain out.

Best,
Sophia

metadata.subject: Physio letter + small adjustments for shifts

artifacts.content:
Period + health planning – November 2024

Stuff to remember for myself (so I stop scrambling last minute):

1. Period + school
- Since the Family Planning NSW clinic in Ashfield visit back in March 2024 and starting the low‑dose hormonal pill in April, things are more regular, but there’s still one really bad day each cycle.
- On that day I basically need ibuprofen + a heat pack and can’t stand for more than 2–3 hours without feeling like my uterus is trying to kill me.
- For the week of 18 Nov: pack ibuprofen (in original box), small heat pack, and a spare pair of black leggings in case.
- Maybe email Ms Taylor if it looks like I’ll miss a double period of PE again.

2. Fainting / food stuff
- Don’t repeat the September 2024 MOBC fainting episode. Eat at least toast + fruit before church.
- Keep muesli bar + electrolyte sachet in bag if on kids’ ministry roster for >3 hours.

3. Anxiety / meds
- Keep taking sertraline 25 mg at night – it’s helping with the social anxiety and panic attacks in crowded places (assemblies at Georges River College Oatley Senior Campus, busy café shifts, church events).
- If panic starts (racing heart, dizzy, hands tingling): breathing exercises from Dr Desai, grounding (5 things I can see, 4 I can touch, etc.).

4. Work + physio
- Respect Daniel Ho’s limits: no lifting heavy crates at Riverside Bean Café, try not to stand more than 4 hours straight.
- Do stretches before/after shifts.

Basically: future me, please don’t ignore this stuff just to “be tough.” It always backfires.
